facts.text: North Haven was first settled around 1650 by William Bradley, became a parish in 1716, and was named North Haven in 1739.
In 1786 North Haven incorporated as a separate town, and by 1798 the Hartford Turnpike and 1813 the Middletown Turnpike linked it to regional trade.
The 1838 arrival of the New Haven and Hartford Railroad brought tracks along the Quinnipiac River, turning North Haven into a rail hub for the era.
The town’s brickmaking industry grew after the Civil War, with I.L. Stiles Co. attracting Irish, German, Italian, and Polish immigrants to work in the I‑L brickworks.
Quinnipiac University’s School of Health Sciences, Law, Education, and Medicine sit on Bassett Road in North Haven, making it a hub for higher education.
